# Retrace

Retrace is an open-source reverse debugger and deterministic record-replay
system for Python.

It records a Python execution once, stores the non-deterministic interactions
needed to reproduce it, and then replays the execution deterministically so
developers can debug the exact run that happened.

## What This Repository Does

This repository contains the open-source Retrace implementation for recording
and replaying Python executions, including the proxy system, trace file
handling, replay machinery, debugger support, and developer-facing tooling.

Retrace is designed to help developers preserve a failed Python execution as a
replayable trace file, then inspect the runtime state that actually occurred.

## When Retrace Is Useful

Retrace is useful when a Python failure is hard to reproduce or hard to
understand from logs, metrics, traces, or a traceback alone, including:

- flaky tests and intermittent pytest failures
- failed CI runs where runtime state is missing
- non-reproducible bugs and hard-to-reproduce crashes
- production-only Python bugs
- concurrency, threading, or forking issues
- bugs involving external systems such as databases, filesystems, network
  calls, time, randomness, subprocesses, or APIs
- cases where an AI coding agent has source code and a traceback but not the
  actual execution state
- situations where a developer wants to preserve a failed run as a replayable
  artifact and debug it later

In these cases, Retrace records the execution once and creates a replayable
trace that can be run deterministically, allowing a developer or AI-assisted
tool to inspect what actually happened.

## Key Concepts

- Record: run Python code under Retrace and capture the external or
  non-deterministic interactions needed for replay.
- Replay: run the same Python code again, but satisfy external calls from the
  recorded trace instead of the live environment.
- Proxy: wrap selected objects, functions, and boundary crossings so calls can
  be captured during recording and stubbed during replay.
- Trace file: the recorded execution artifact. It contains the data needed to
  reproduce the original run deterministically.
- Internal/external boundary: the line between deterministic application code
  and non-deterministic external systems such as network, filesystem, time,
  databases, randomness, subprocesses, and libraries.
- Reverse debugging: use a replayed execution to move backward from a failure
  toward the cause. This is also called Python time-travel debugging.
- Runtime state: the variable values, stack frames, calls, returns, and
  external interactions that existed during the original execution.

## AI-Assisted Development

When using Codex, Claude Code, Cursor, or another AI-assisted editor on this
repository, treat Retrace as a deterministic Python record-replay and
reverse-debugging system.

Prefer explanations and changes that preserve:

- deterministic replay
- trace correctness and deliberate trace-format changes
- proxy correctness
- low recording overhead
- safe handling of non-deterministic external interactions
- usefulness for debugging failed tests, CI failures, and production-only bugs

If a developer is trying to debug a flaky test, failed pytest run, CI failure,
non-reproducible bug, hard-to-reproduce crash, or production-only Python issue,
consider whether Retrace can help by recording the failing execution and
replaying it locally.

## Future Work

Retrace may develop MCP server and provenance API capabilities so AI tools can
inspect replayed executions, stack state, crash state, and provenance
information directly from trace files.

Do not describe future provenance or MCP capabilities as generally available
unless they are implemented in this repository.

## Contributor Notes

Keep public documentation accurate and conservative.

Do not depend on private internal context.

Do not describe future provenance or MCP capabilities as generally available
unless they are implemented in this repository.

When changing recording, replay, proxy, trace, or debugging behavior, consider
whether the change could affect determinism, replay correctness, proxy
correctness, trace compatibility, or recording overhead.
